sa1_e_notes_SP1IFoA202504_2_ii:
granulation of risks
consider the new pillars used by the regulator

build risk profile at product level - what does this mean?
Risk Profile - A risk profile is a comprehensive, structured snapshot of all the risks a company is exposed to — capturing what those risks are, how large they are, how likely they are to materialise, and what impact they would have on the company if they did. It is essentially the company's risk identity card — a complete picture of its risk landscape at a given point in time.

The Key Principle

Granulation means moving from "we have risk" → to "we have X amount of morbidity risk on product Y, driven by Z assumption, which requires K amount of capital to cover at a 99.5% confidence level."

That specificity is what the new capital regime demands and rewards.

In Practical Terms for an Insurance Company
Instead of saying "we have underwriting risk", granulation means going deeper:

Underwriting Risk (Morbidity) broken down into:
Risk of higher-than-expected claims on hospital cash plans
Risk of claim inflation on critical illness covers
Risk of adverse experience on group medical vs individual medical
Lapse/persistency risk per product class

Market Risk broken down into:
Interest rate risk on reserves
Credit/default risk on bond holdings
Equity concentration risk
Currency mismatch risk

Operational Risk broken down into:
IT system failure risk
Fraud risk
Claims processing error risk
Regulatory non-compliance risk

Expense Risk broken down into:
Risk that unit expenses exceed pricing assumptions
Risk of expense overruns due to inflation

Reasons for volatility:
Volatility in incidence rates:
•	Change in composition – age/gender/geo mix – M&A, restrct, econ cycle
•	Avg age increasing – higher vol in incid rates
•	Wellness/preventive initiatives – e.g. screening camps – sudden surge clms
•	Accidents/pandemic/catastrophe – concentration risk high in group
Volatility in claim amounts:
•	Med inflation – higher than anticipated
•	Med/tech advancements – new diseases/treatments – unexpected costs
•	Provider behavior – little control – billing/treatment protocol changes
•	Util pattern changes – between 3 benefit types – over/under
•	Clms mgmt – lenient – pay otherwise denied clms
•	Large clms – unlimited benefits – periodic large adds vol
Mitigation:
•	Insurance – some/all benefits – fixed prem – transfer vol risk to insurer
•	Redesign benefits – intro caps/ceilings – max liab per event limited
•	Coinsurance/deductible – cost sharing – overall clm cost reduces
•	Stricter eligibility – e.g. perm employees only
•	Stringent clms mgmt – only eligible clms paid
•	Wellness/preventive focus – reduce large clms – early diagnosis
•	Network mgmt – fix treatment cost – encourage network use – contain outgo

Story for Recall

"Workers in the neighbouring country did dangerous jobs for low wages — they couldn't afford full IP. But an accident-only product is inherently cheaper than full IP (no sickness claims), putting it within reach. The state picked up sickness claims but left accident victims with nothing. So the government backed a product to fill that gap, employers were told to offer it, and banks tied it to mortgages. The product was dead simple to buy — no long medicals. Then a string of high-profile accidents hit the headlines, insurers ran big campaigns, and since it was the only product of its kind, it swept the market."

Cheaper than regular IP -> Dangerous jobs → Low wages so cannot afford full ip → State gap → Govt backed → Employer mandate → Simple → sig portion in good health -> or exposed to higher risk of accident -> Accidents in news → Only product.
